danger_signals.visual_form: Animated jungle wildlife and antagonist characters (Shere Khan, Kaa)
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring avoidance or projectile combat

danger_signals.visual_form: Distinctive large character sprite appearing at level transitions
danger_signals.meaning: Major narrative obstacle; projectile-only vulnerability signals combat-required state
Failure condition; communicates urgency and restricted exploration window

danger_signals.visual_form: Jungle terrain obstacles requiring vine-swinging and platforming navigation
danger_signals.meaning: Physical danger requiring precise movement; failure results in damage/death

safety_signals.visual_form: Named NPC characters (Bagheera, Baloo, King Louie) rendered distinctly from enemies
safety_signals.meaning: Level completion objective; safe interaction point marking stage finish

safety_signals.visual_form: Gems scattered throughout levels, banana projectiles, fruit items
safety_signals.meaning: Progress markers; gems quantify advancement toward character-finding objective

safety_signals.visual_form: Visual exit marker (SNES version alternative to finding friendly character)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe passage forward; marks level boundary

safety_signals.visual_form: Invincibility masks, coconuts, double banana shots, boomerang bananas
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary threat mitigation; visual abundance indicates safety window

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Human village destination; represents civilization/safety state
Scattered throughout jungle; mark safe zones within hostile landscape

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Antagonist character sprites; Shere Khan (tiger) and Kaa (hypnotist snake) as named bosses
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Standard enemies versus distinctive boss sprites for Shere Khan encounters
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Concentrated in hostile jungle areas; signal danger zones

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Jungle environment with scattered friendly NPCs and collectibles; relatively sparse enemy density
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mowgli begins in wolf home; movement is exploratory; no immediate combat pressure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Increased enemy sprite density; Shere Khan appears as boss encounter; jungle environment darkens or intensifies visually (inferred from narrative framing)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Combat becomes necessary; avoidance patterns shift to confrontation; boss fights require sustained projectile attacks

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Chapter transitions with story descriptions; level-to-level progression through jungle stages; gem collection drives forward momentum
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mowgli must collect incremental gem counts (8-12 depending on difficulty) before character-finding objective unlocks

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Friendly character appearance; exit sign visibility; absence of immediate enemy sprites in close proximity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can reach completion objective; no projectile attacks required; level finishes

environmental_storytelling.example: Baloo, Bagheera, and King Louie placed in specific level locations as find-targets; their presence signals narrative waypoints in Mowgli's journey toward the village
Each chapter opens with narrative context describing plot progression and stage objectives; verbal storytelling anchors visual exploration
Shere Khan appears as named boss every other level; his presence escalates threat narrative; projectile-only vulnerability communicates his danger level
Vine-swinging, platforming, and environmental navigation mirror Mowgli's physical journey away from wolf home toward human civilization; terrain difficulty increases narrative tension
Banana projectile baseline upgraded to double bananas and boomerang variants; powerup collection visually communicates Mowgli's growing capability to survive the hunt

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Approaches friendly NPCs; movement slows or halts at character location; animation transitions to completion/success state
Rapid movement; projectile firing animation loops; jumping/dodging patterns intensify in proximity to enemies
Appears as distinct large sprite; immune to jumping attacks; only projectile impacts register damage; indicates escalated threat tier
Wandering sprite animation; predictable movement patterns; avoidable or defeatable with basic projectiles
Stationary sprites scattered throughout level; collection audio-visual feedback (score increment) signals progress toward character-finding threshold
Stationary or minimal animation; awaiting player approach; visual distinctness from enemies (animation frame quality, character recognition)

notes: The Jungle Book SNES version uses a primarily single-character (Mowgli) progression system with faction identity rooted in source material (Disney film). World state is communicated through: (1) enemy density and boss encounters indicating hunt escalation, (2) gem collection creating quantified progress feedback, (3) character-as-objective placement marking narrative waypoints, and (4) environmental hazards requiring skill-based navigation. Danger is signaled through sprite distinctness (enemies vs. allies), projectile-immunity patterns (boss-tier threats), and time pressure. The game lacks faction-based visual distinction beyond enemy/ally binary; factions are narratively defined (Mowgli's allies vs. predators/antagonists) rather than visually marked through color or heraldry. Political situation is implicit in plot (Shere Khan hunt, village return) rather than dynamically manifested in world visuals.
